title: Zeus: The Cloud-Gatherer
content: Zeus is the sky and thunder god in ancient Greek religion, who rules as king of the gods of Mount Olympus. His name is cognate with the first element of his Roman equivalent, Jupiter. Zeus is the child of Cronus and Rhea, and the youngest of his siblings to be born, though sometimes reckoned the eldest as the others required disgorging from Cronus's stomach. In most traditions, he is married to Hera, by whom he is usually said to have fathered Ares, Hebe, and Hephaestus. At the oracle of Dodona, his consort was said to be Dione, by whom the Iliad states that he fathered Aphrodite. Extensive commentary on Zeus surveys his cult centers, epithets, votive types, thunder imagery, and intertextual reception across archaic and classical poetry. title: Athena: Parthenos
content: Athena or Athene, often given the epithet Pallas, is an ancient Greek goddess associated with wisdom, handicraft, and warfare who was later syncretized with the Roman goddess Minerva. Athena was regarded as the patron and protectress of various cities across Greece, particularly the city of Athens, from which she most likely received her name. The Parthenon on the Acropolis of Athens is dedicated to her. Her major symbols include owls, olive trees, snakes, and the Gorgoneion. In art, she is generally depicted wearing a helmet and holding a spear. Extensive archaeological and textual work explores Athena's civic cult, the Parthenon program, Panathenaic festival practices, dedications from artisans, and her enduring role in Greek identity narratives. title: The Jade Emperor: Yuhuang Dadi
content: The Jade Emperor is the representation of the first god in Chinese culture and is considered the ruler of Heaven and all realms of existence below including that of Man and Hell. He is one of the most important gods in the Chinese traditional religion pantheon. In Taoist theology, he is the assistant of the Three Pure Ones, who are the three primordial emanations of the Tao. The Jade Emperor's birthday is said to be the ninth day of the first lunar month. He is often depicted in art as a middle-aged man wearing a ceremonial robe and a crown, sitting on a throne in the celestial palace. Extensive comparative studies examine the Jade Emperor's bureaucratic cosmology, ritual calendars, palace imagery, and syncretic incorporation into folk practice. title: Sun Wukong: The Monkey King
content: Sun Wukong, known as the Monkey King, is a legendary figure best known as one of the main characters in the 16th-century Chinese novel Journey to the West. He possesses immense strength and is able to support the weight of two celestial mountains on his shoulders while running at the speed of a meteor. Wukong is capable of 72 transformations, which allow him to change into various animals and objects. He is a skilled fighter, capable of defeating the best warriors of heaven. His primary weapon is the Ruyi Jingu Bang, a staff that can change its size according to his will. Extensive narrative analysis covers Sun Wukong's episodic transformations, his trickster motifs, ritual associations in folk religion, and his afterlife within visual culture. title: Pluto: Lord of the Underworld
content: Pluto was the ruler of the underworld in Roman mythology. The deity was earlier known as Hades in Greek mythology, which became more common as the name of the underworld itself. Pluto represents a more positive concept of the god who presides over the afterlife; he was also associated with the mineral wealth of the earth, hence the name Pluto (from the Greek word for 'wealth'). His wife was Proserpina, whom the Romans identified with the Greek Persephone. Pluto was one of the three sons of Saturn and Ops, alongside Jupiter and Neptune. Archaeological and literary materials detail funerary cults, chthonic offerings, subterranean iconography, and the economic metaphors linking the underworld to mineral wealth. title: Guan Yin: Goddess of Mercy
content: Guan Yin is the Buddhist bodhisattva associated with compassion and is the East Asian representation of Avalokiteśvara. She is commonly known as the 'Goddess of Mercy' in English. Guan Yin is revered by both Buddhists and Taoists. She is often depicted in white robes, standing atop a lotus or a dragon, carrying a water jar and a willow branch. Legend says that she vowed not to enter the heavenly realms until all living beings had been completed in their enlightenment and liberated from the cycle of birth and death. Extensive devotional literature, temple iconography, and cross-cultural transformations document her adaptation across sects and regions. title: The Twelve Labors of Heracles
content: The Twelve Labors of Heracles are a series of episodes concerning a very great penance carried out by Heracles, the greatest of the Greek heroes. They were accomplished over 12 years at the service of King Eurystheus. The labors include: 1. Slaying the Nemean Lion. 2. Slaying the nine-headed Lernaean Hydra. 3. Capturing the Ceryneian Hind. 4. Capturing the Erymanthian Boar. 5. Cleaning the Augean stables in a single day. 6. Slaying the Stymphalian birds. 7. Capturing the Cretan Bull. 8. Stealing the Mares of Diomedes. 9. Obtaining the girdle of Hippolyta. 10. Obtaining the cattle of the giant Geryon. 11. Stealing the apples of the Hesperides. 12. Capturing and bringing back Cerberus. Extended narrative and iconographic studies analyze how each labor functions as a moral test, a cosmological ordering, and a claim to heroic identity across Greek and later Roman adaptations. Extended narrative and iconographic studies analyze how each labor functions as a moral test, a cosmological ordering, and a claim to heroic identity across Greek and later Roman adaptations. title: Nüwa: The Creator of Humanity
content: Nüwa is the mother goddess of Chinese mythology, credited with creating humanity and repairing the Pillar of Heaven. According to legend, she molded the first humans out of yellow clay. Finding the process too tedious, she dipped a rope into the mud and flicked it, creating the common people, while the hand-molded ones became the nobility. When a great war between the gods damaged the sky, Nüwa smelted five-colored stones to patch the hole and used the legs of a giant tortoise to support the heavens, preventing the world's destruction. Comparative mythological studies consider Nüwa's creation motifs alongside other culture-origin myths and examine ritual responses to cosmic disorder in early Chinese sources.
